narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a model trained on data from thousands of students like you, looking at factors such as early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPA, family income, and whether you'd study full-time or part-time, to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you specifically, the model estimated a 48% probability that you might fail the bar exam initially, which crossed the threshold for denial since the school prioritizes applicants predicted to pass with high confidence. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the lower part of your class—much lower than the typical student's mid-range standing—signaling to the model a potential struggle in advanced coursework that often correlates with bar success. This outweighed other influences, like your lower-middle family income bracket, which actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing compared to students from higher-income backgrounds. Your LSAT score of 31, below the average of around 37, and your Year 1 decile of 3, also lower than typical, contributed modestly toward expecting a pass, as did your undergraduate GPA of 2.3 versus the usual 3.2. However, choosing part-time study—uncommon, as over 90% of students go full-time—pushed a bit toward the failure side, likely because the model sees it as a risk for divided focus. Overall, your profile stands out as having stronger early academics and test scores than your later school performance suggested, putting you below the dataset's typical successful student who maintains consistent high deciles, solid LSATs, and full-time commitment. While this decision feels tough, especially given barriers many Black women face in education, it's not the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking ways to improve late-stage grades through targeted tutoring, or exploring part-time programs with proven bar passage support. You might also strengthen your application by highlighting any personal achievements or hardships that build resilience. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas and reapplying stronger. Keep pushing forward; your journey to law is worth it.
